data.name: ArchitectureIntroduction
data.value: Here is the architecture of the website you're browsing on.

data.name: ArchitectureMe2
data.value: - a design based on popular sources in accordance with the project's constraints

data.name: ArchitectureMicroservice
data.value: This is an app based on microservices where two domain-driven design (DDD) patterns are implemented: Command and Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS) et event sourcing.

data.name: ArchitectureReference1
data.value: The code is inspired from many references like

data.name: CommandCommandStepValidation
data.value: Simple rules validation: mark value between 0 and 5.

data.name: CommandDomainStepValidation
data.value: Business rules validation: the profile is recommended but the mark is below 3.

data.name: CommandsDescription
data.value: This sequence diagram is a simplification of the writing process.

data.name: CommandSendStepValidation
data.value: Front-end validation of the form: required recommendation's field.

data.name: CommandsExample
data.value: Let's start by creating a command to add a rate about the website:

data.name: CQRSBenefits
data.value: This concept has many advantages: maintenance (responsibility segregation), scalability of read and write, performance of read operations, command validation and business rules respect...

data.name: CQRSDefinition
data.value: CQRS is an architecture pattern where queries ("read") and commands ("write") are separated.

data.name: CQRSDiagram
data.value: This sequence diagram is a simplification of the write process then the read process triggered by REST queries.

data.name: CQRSExample
data.value: What's better than a practical example? Here we go!

data.name: CQRSService
data.value: Here, the "Rating" service applies CQRS to handle the scores of the visitors of this website.

data.name: EventSourcingBenefits
data.value: It gives a complete history, ease the debugging and temporal requests, and can rebuild the state of the system.

data.name: EventSourcingDefinition1
data.value: Event sourcing is a pattern where state changes are saved like a sequence of immutable events rather than changing the current state directly. A very concrete example is a

data.name: EventSourcingDefinition2
data.value: where the current state would be the result of all the moves of the players (events).

data.name: EventSourcingReadResult
data.value: The read database is updated on each event to compute the 3 top reasons which make you read this:

data.name: EventSourcingService
data.value: Here, the "Insight" service applies CQRS + event sourcing to handle what you expect from my profile and why you are on this website.

data.name: EventSourcingWrite
data.value: Every event is store in an append-only database.

data.name: EventSourcingWriteBenefits
data.value: Why it's great? Because the current state is known but you can also replay all the events or get the state at a specific moment.

data.name: EventSourcingWriteExample
data.value: Let's see what it means with this example:

data.name: EventSourcingWriteExampleInstructions
data.value: Please choose one or more reasons that lead you here, drop them in the zone and order them by pertinence:

data.name: EventSourcingWriteExampleResult
data.value: This is the result in the event store:

data.name: EventSourcingWriteIllustration
data.value: This is useful whatever the field is: what were the treatments of a patient? How where the state of the drones before they crashed? What did the player when the bug happened?...

data.name: IntentHelpingDescription
data.value: Helping Valérian for free because I'm nice

data.name: MicroserviceAPI
data.value: A gateway (YARP) to avoid a direct access and coupling the services with the interface.

data.name: MicroserviceAPIContent
data.value: This avoids coupling the services with the interface and also secure the app with a single entrypoint.

data.name: MicroserviceBackend
data.value: Two microservices which communicate with RabbitMQ and use their own databases (EventStoreDB, MongoDB).

data.name: MicroserviceBackendContent
data.value: Two microservices which communicate with RabbitMQ and use their own databases (EventStoreDB, MongoDB).

data.name: MicroserviceDescription
data.value: Here is a short description of the architecture of this project:

data.name: MicroserviceDocker
data.value: The apps are containerized with Docker and launched through Docker Compose.

data.name: MicroserviceFrontend
data.value: A user interface developed with Blazor Server and MudBlazor.

data.name: MicroserviceFrontendContent
data.value: I'm also comfortable with Bootstrap and I've done a ReactJS website.

data.name: MicroserviceReference
data.value: My developments are widely inspired from this Microsoft guide which helped me to understand and implement microservices apps:

data.name: QueriesExample
data.value: Let's see now the last scores:
